nativeRange.habitat: The species inhabits rocky reefs and adjacent boulder fields in the southern part of Lake Malawi, spending most of its time inside or immediately around caves and crevices between rock piles. It rarely ventures into open water.

decorPreferences: large rock piles forming caves and crevices
open sandy substrate lanes for mid-water swimming
flat rocks or slate as potential spawning sites
minimal or no planting (not part of natural habitat)

diet.notes: In the wild, O. lithobates is an opportunistic scavenger and detritivore (a species that feeds on organic debris and waste), picking up the droppings of herbivorous rock-dwelling fish and gleaning small invertebrates from the substrate. In the aquarium, a protein-rich diet best reflects these habits: high-quality cichlid pellets or sticks make a good staple, supplemented with frozen or live mysis shrimp, krill, chopped prawns, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Feeding once or twice daily in modest portions is generally recommended. Mammalian meats such as beef heart and other fat-rich foods should be avoided, as they are associated with bloat in Lake Malawi cichlids.

goodWith: peaceful non-mbuna Lake Malawi haplochromines of similar size
avoidWith: fish with similar yellow-head or sulphur-stripe coloration (triggers male aggression)
small fish or fry that may be predated
very large or highly aggressive haps (Nimbochromis, Tyrannochromis)

imageCandidates.notes: Steven Viemeister; 1024x683. Sharp, well-framed live specimen in a Commons category 'Otopharynx lithobates'; shows the iridescent blue body and orange-red fin edging. Sub-dominant/developing male rather than peak sulphur-yellow-headed nuptial colour, but the only CC live photo available.

summary: The Sulphur-Head Hap is a striking Lake Malawi cichlid whose breeding males display an iridescent blue body topped by a vivid sulphur-yellow blaze along the head and dorsal fin. A committed rock-dweller occupying the transitional zone between mbuna and open-water hap habitat, it is a maternal mouthbrooder best kept as a harem in a spacious Malawi biotope with other peaceful haps.
careNotes:
Otopharynx lithobates, commonly called the Sulphur-Head Hap or Yellow Blaze, is endemic to rocky shorelines in the southern part of Lake Malawi, where it spends nearly all of its time inside or immediately around boulder piles — a habit it retains faithfully in captivity. Unlike many open-water haplochromines, it is rarely seen swimming freely in the water column and relies heavily on rockwork for shelter and territory.

A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is recommended to accommodate the territorial dynamics of a harem group (one male, three or more females). The aquascape should consist of substantial rock piles arranged into caves and overhangs, with open sandy lanes between them for mid-water movement. Flat rocks or smooth slate pieces serve as preferred spawning platforms.

Water chemistry should reflect Lake Malawi conditions: temperature 24–28 °C, pH 7.6–8.8, and moderate to hard water (gH 10–25). Stability is as important as absolute values; sudden drops in pH or temperature can trigger stress and disease. Weekly partial water changes of 25–50 % are strongly advised to keep nitrates below 40 ppm.

Breeding males are among the most visually striking of the Lake Malawi haplochromines: the three dark lateral spots on the flanks become masked by a vivid neon-blue body pattern, while the head and most of the dorsal fin turn a deep sulphur-yellow-orange. Non-dominant and female specimens are a subdued grey-beige with faint lateral spotting.

Because the species name lithobates literally means “rock-dweller” (from Greek lithos + bates), aquarists should furnish generous rockwork even for single specimens. The male's bright coloration makes it a target for aggression from similarly coloured fish; avoid tankmates bearing yellow dorsal or head patterns. O. lithobates is generally considered unsuitable with boisterous mbuna, which tend to harass it persistently.

Avoid mammalian-derived proteins (beef heart, etc.) and high-fat foods, which are associated with bloat (Malawi bloat) in Lake Malawi cichlids generally.
breedingNotes: O. lithobates is a maternal mouthbrooder. Males clear a flat rock or smooth substrate patch as a spawning site and display intensely to attract females. After spawning, the female immediately takes the eggs into her buccal cavity. Incubation lasts approximately 18–28 days at typical aquarium temperatures (25–27 °C), during which the female does not feed. Brood size is typically 25–50 fry. Upon release, fry are free-swimming and large enough to accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and finely crushed pellets. The female can be separated to a dedicated holding tank shortly before releasing fry to protect them from predation by tankmates. A harem ratio of one male to at least three females is recommended to disperse the male’s mating attention and reduce female exhaustion.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Otopharynx lithobates
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (16 cm TL), temperature (24–28 °C), pH (7.6–8.8), hardness (10–25 °H), minimum tank (160 L / 90 cm), distribution (southern Lake Malawi rocky sites), diet guidance, breeding details (maternal mouthbrooder, 25–50 fry, ~4-week incubation), compatibility guidance (avoid mbuna), and habitat description. Tank minimum in this file raised to 300 L / 150 cm for a harem group per cross-source guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Otopharynx lithobates
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males 16.0 cm TL, females 10.0 cm TL), temperature (24–27 °C), depth range (0–10 m), native range (southern Lake Malawi, Malawi), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes). Wild diet described as detritivore/scavenger feeding on droppings of herbivorous species; trophic level 2.0.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Sulphurhead Hap
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (15–16 cm), temperature (24–27 °C), pH (7.6–8.4), hardness (8–20 °N), carnivore/insectivore diet classification, 8–10-year lifespan, harem breeding structure, and tank-length guidance (120 cm minimum for single male; 150–180 cm for community). Compatibility notes (avoid similarly marked fish, mbuna) cross-checked.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity.com — Sulphur-Crested Lithobate
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed temperature (24–27 °C), maternal mouthbrooder status (~3-week incubation), scavenger wild diet feeding on droppings of herbivorous rock-dwelling fish, semi-aggressive male hierarchy, and 120 cm minimum tank recommendation. Carbonate hardness reference (~250 ppm) noted.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red-top Aristochromis / Otopharynx lithobates
additional.notes: Trade-name and synonym cross-check; confirmed common synonyms (Red-top Aristochromis, Sulphur-Head Hap) and compatibility guidance for Malawi biotope communities.

fish.breedingNotes: The Sulphur-Head Hap is a maternal mouthbrooder. A harem ratio of one male to at least three females is generally recommended to distribute the male's mating attention. The male clears a flat rock or smooth substrate patch as a spawning site and displays to attract females; after spawning, the female takes the eggs into her buccal cavity and broods them for 18–28 days without feeding. Fry are free-swimming on release and large enough to accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Moving the holding female to a separate tank before she releases fry helps protect them from predation by tankmates.
